up.method: Table 1: fast-growth mode consumes glucose+pyruvate; slow-growth mode consumes acetate+lactate (rates modeled only)

sec.method: Table 1: produces butyrate (both modes), acetate, lactate (fast mode); no numeric flux reported

growth_records.cite: Garza et al. Emergence of alternative states in a synthetic human gut microbial community. Nature Communications (2025). DOI:10.1038/s41467-025-67036-5. [PMC12789478]
growth_records.snippet: Roseburia intestinalis generates butyrate through the reverse β-oxidation pathway ... efficiently consumed glucose and pyruvate, producing butyrate, acetate, and lactate ... feed delivered at an approximate rate of 0.04 h−1.
growth_records.notes: mu=D=0.04 h-1 (calculated, chemostat steady-state coexistence in control). doubling_time=ln2/0.04=17.3 h. A separate pH-landscape series fixed dilution at 0.067 h-1 (=> mu=0.067 if at steady state). R. intestinalis has fast-growth (glucose/pyruvate->butyrate+acetate+lactate) and slow-growth (acetate/lactate->butyrate) modes. WC broth composition not enumerated (stated=false); trehalose 0.71 mM only quantified metabolite. Uptake/secretion qualitative (Table 1). Temperature/pH not stated in extractable text.

up.method: main carbon source, depleted by 24 h; Fig. 2b, no numeric rate
main carbon source, depleted by 24 h; Fig. 2b
consumed during slow-growth (post-glucose) diauxic phase, converted to butyrate; Fig. 2b, qualitative
consumed in slow-growth phase in some replicates; Fig. S3, qualitative

sec.method: fermentation product; significantly increased in slow-growth phase (HPLC), Fig. 2b

growth_records.cite: Liu et al. Starvation responses impact interaction dynamics of human gut bacteria Bacteroides thetaiotaomicron and Roseburia intestinalis. The ISME Journal (2023). DOI:10.1038/s41396-023-01501-1. [PMC10579405]
growth_records.snippet: R. intestinalis in monoculture produced lactate, formate, acetate and butyrate from glucose and pyruvate, corresponding to a pH decrease from 6.7 to 5.8 at 24 h and reached a maximal density of 7.8 x 10^5 viable cells/ul... a drop in viable
growth_records.notes: Batch anaerobic monoculture in WC (no mucin). Growth = viable cells/ul by flow cytometry. Max 7.8e5 viable cells/ul at 24 h; pH 6.7->5.8 at 24 h. After glucose/pyruvate depletion at 24 h, enters slow-growth (diauxic) mode consuming acetate+lactate to make butyrate, pH rising 5.7->6.0; viable cells 1.8-3.8e5 from 40-120 h (two experiments). No mu or doubling time reported; not computable (no t=0 viable-cell density; substrate/product values only in figures; biomass in cells/ul not gDW). Diauxic shift corroborated by RNA-seq up-regulation of lactate dehydrogenase (RIL182_00335, 01719) and butyryl-CoA:acetate CoA-transferase (RIL182_00413) between 12 and 48 h. WC full composition not stated; glucose+pyruvate named as carbon sources. 37 C, 170 rpm, initial pH 6.7.

growth_records.medium: Wilkins-Chalgren Anaerobe Broth (WC; Oxoid) with porcine mucin beads

up.method: carbon source; growth impeded by mucin; Fig. 2e
consumed in slow-growth phase -> butyrate; qualitative

growth_records.cite: Liu et al. Starvation responses impact interaction dynamics of human gut bacteria Bacteroides thetaiotaomicron and Roseburia intestinalis. The ISME Journal (2023). DOI:10.1038/s41396-023-01501-1. [PMC10579405]
growth_records.snippet: the growth of R. intestinalis on glucose/pyruvate seems to be impeded by mucin, demonstrated by ... lower maximum of 4.5 x 10^5 viable cells/ul at 36 h ... a much lower density of 0.3-0.5 x 10^5 viable cells/ul than in the absence of mucin.
growth_records.notes: Batch anaerobic RI monoculture in WC + mucin beads. Planktonic max 4.5e5 viable cells/ul at 36 h (lower than the 7.8e5 without mucin - mucin impedes RI growth on glucose/pyruvate). Slow-growth phase density 0.3-0.5e5 cells/ul. Bead-attached max 1.1e5 viable cells/ul at 32 h. Products lactate/formate/acetate/butyrate; slow-growth acetate->butyrate conversion. No mu/doubling time reported or computable (cells/ul, no t=0 density; figure-only fluxes). Mucin bead composition stated; WC base not stated. 37 C, 170 rpm.
